content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies where the reboot is better than the original?

**Score:** 223
**Body:** Currently watching Total Recall (2012) and boy is it awful 😂. I’m a big fan of the original Total Recall. 

I was quick to write of reboots as all together bad, then realized another of my favorites (DUNE) is waaaay better than the original. 

Seems like a pretty rare fluke that budgets, casts, etc align in such a way that Hollywood produces a better product decades removed from the source material.  

What other remakes are better than the original? 


### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The two amazing body-horror 80s movie remakes did this. The Fly and The Thing. Both remakes of B movies from the 50s. (The originals are decent, just nothing like the amazing remakes).


The Thomas Crown Affair remake (Piers Brosnan) is probably better than the original (Steve McQueen).


Same with Ocean's 11.
  - Toss The Blob in there, too.
  - Invasion of the Body Snatchers with Donald Sutherland is also a *chef’s kiss* remake
  - I was going to say The Thomas Crown Affair! The remake is still one of my favorite movies. Rene Russo and Pierce Brosnan have better chemistry than Faye Dunaway and Steve McQueen, imo.
2. Dredd (2012)… and it’s not close!
3. Heat (1995) is much better than L.A. Takedown (1989), a film a lot of people probably don't even know exists.
4. The Thing (1982), The Fly (1986), Airplane! (1980), Little Shop of Horrors (1986), The Blob (1988).
5. Reboot implies a franchise. You probably mean remakes

True Grit (2010) is among my favorite films. And I say this as a person that used to hate Westerns
6. Ocean's 11
7. I always thought the 70’s Invasion of the Body Snatchers was better than the 50’s film.

## 2. Any great critically acclaimed movies from 80s to present that I should watch blind

**Score:** 37
**Body:** it doesn’t have to have some twist or anything but i just want a list of movies from 80s to present day to watch completely blind. i prefer watching movies without knowing anything about it but the problem is that ill run into a lot of mid movies that waste my time 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. They Live
  - Double feature with THE THING. 

Would looooove to go into that one blind.
2. The Lives of Others
3. The Last Emperor
4. Amadeus. 1984, Directed by Milos Forman.

About the wild life and times of Mozart. 

It stars Tom Hulce & F. Murray Abraham. Excellent acting from both, worth a watch!
5. To Live & Die in LA
6. "Brazil"

(40th anniversary this year, and unfortunately as timely as ever)
7. - Silence of the Lambs
- The Lives of Others
- Incendies
- El Secreto de sus Ojos
- LA Confidential
8. Check out Videodrome (1983). Best to go in blind.
9. Repo Man (1984) - Has Harry Dean Stanton.  This movie is fucking cool.

Who Framed Roger Rabbit (1988) - Nothing in this movie is computer generated and it still hasn't been topped; this film is the definition of movie magic.

## 3. Hamnet (2025)

**Score:** 33
**Body:** I read the book, so I thought I was prepared, and I had high expectations but *boy* did this one deliver and some. It is easily one of the most emotional movies I’ve ever seen but also one of the most moving. It explores all the spectrum of feeling and humanity that Shakespeare was able to integrate into his works. But I also think you don’t have even have to be remotely familiar with Shakespeare to enjoy this movie. The acting alone is so compelling and captivating! Do yourself a favor and watch a piece of art that will move you, like this movie! 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Just watched it this evening. I’m so blown away by the performances. I’ve never read any Shakespeare works, but there’s something about this movie and story that’s so captivating. I thoroughly enjoyed it and Jessie Buckley is a powerhouse of an actor. Never been more enamored and mesmerized by someone in a role.
2. The book wrecked me so I've been nervous about watching this one, but you might have just convinced me. Sometimes when adaptations get the emotional core right it hits even harder than reading it.
  - Be nervous. It took the emotions of the book and put them on 1,000 but I loved it. The actress who plays Agnes better win best actress. I was worried because the prose of the book is so moving but the movie delivered, and the kid actors did SO GOOD!
3. I've really been wanting to watch this but wasn't sure if I would be able to understand it as I am not familiar with Shakespeare besides the 90's adaptations of some of his work (I think?).. the previews just look so dang good and I want all of it!! Reading this makes me very happy. Thank you for sharing and making someone like me, who doesn't feel intelligent enough to be able to watch it and follow, feel like I can sit and enjoy this without stressing if I am going to know what is going on or not.
  - No you don’t have to know any Shakespeare at all. It’s very well done. It’s more about family dynamics and relationships and grief I think.
4. 🐷🥅
5. I finally got off the waitlist for the book at my library! I watched the movie first and really loved it.
6. Yep, completely agree, great movie.  I went into it blind, figuring I'd walk out if it was bad, but it got me from the start.  I've recommended it to several friends.
7. I'm hesitant to watch as it looks like Oscar bait
  - Its absolutely not oscar bait lol

## 4. Movies about a dying indursty

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Hey guys, do you have any movie that captured the last days of an industry before it dies/at least becomes way less significant than it used to? Something like Radio Days where it captured the last days of radio being a #1 media in America before TVs took over or Babylon where silent films dying when talkies/films that featured sound became common. Thank you!  

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Singin' in the Rain (1952) is a musical romance comedy about the last days of silent films, and the early talkies.
  - The Artist with a similar theme
2. Boogie Nights
3. Be Kind Rewind
4. [The Full Monty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Full_Monty) set in Sheffield as the steel industry dies and a group of ex-workers find a way to spend their time and inject a little joy.
5. This may fit,

Kodachrome (2017)

Matt and his father, who is terminally ill, embark on a road trip to Kansas to process a Kodachrome film before Kodak's last laboratory shuts down permanently.
6. Tommy Boy is the funniest movie ever made about the Rust Belt and dying automotive industry
7. "Roger and Me" 
8. **Blackberry** (2023)

A deep felt story about when you did nothing wrong but failed because someone changed the paradigm.
9. Slap Shot

## 5. Please recommend some deeply underrated comedies.

**Score:** 28
**Body:** I'm talking movies with less than 50% on Rotten Tomatoes you'd defend with your life. Weird-ass films you found in the bargain bin of a Best Buy that had you in stitches. Something everyone rolls their eyes at but you hold dearly.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kung Pow! Enter The Fist
2. * Just Friends (2005) - 43%
* Grandma’s Boy (2006) - 15% 
* Strange Wilderness (2008) - 2%
* Johnny Dangerously (1984) - 44% 
* Wet Hot American Summer (2001) - 39%
* Better Living Through Chemistry (2014) - 24%
  - Those fargin’ bastitches gave it a 44%? ???
    - Iceholes!
  - Grandma’s boy is hilarious
3. Orange County (2002). Written by Mike White who also has a cameo. 47% on RT. A brilliant movie in my opinion. It lives somewhere between Arrested Development and the White Lotus.
4. Joe Dirt, Land of the Lost and The Campaign.
5. Don’t hear Masterminds talked about much but I enjoyed it.
6. Death to smoochie
7. Dirty Work (1998)

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Christopher Nolan doesn't understand why The Dark Knight Rises has been seen as a right-wing film:

**Score:** 1102
**Body:** 
“Of all of my films, The Dark Knight Rises is the one that’s been pushed and pulled in the weirdest number of directions. I think you have to go out of your way to look at the film and attribute to it right-wing characteristics. If anything, it’s specifically left-wing. When people listen to Bane and say, ‘He sounds like Donald Trump’ or that Donald Trump sounds like him, well, it’s about a demagogue. He’s the bad guy. What was I afraid of when I made The Dark Knight Rises? I was afraid of demagoguery. Turns out I was right to be afraid. The film was not supposed to be political. It’s not intended to be, it’s about primal fears.”

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What I recall is that it coincided with Occupy Wall Street and the movie seemed to be equating Bane's populist support with Occupy. Iirc, Rises was in production before Occupy happened, so it's a coincidence.
  - Yeah spot on. Nolan started writing way before Occupy blew up, total coincidence. Bane’s mob is more French Revolution chaos than peaceful tents in parks. People just saw what they wanted and ran with it lol.
    - It’s the fundamental weakness of the film, that Bane and his gang lack any coherent message.

In the Dark Knight Nolan had a nihilistic villain who “just wanted to watch the world burn”.  It felt a bit like he was just trying to replicate that.  But Joker and his goons are mentally ill, it’s their nature.  Bane is supposed to be hyper intelligent and his gang are motivated by self interest.
      - Bane literally doesn't have a message. In-universe, all of his political messaging is just bullshit that he's intentionally spreading to sow chaos, while his real agenda is just to nuke the entire city.

So the lack of coherence definitely isn't accidental. But it does kind of carry the implications that movements that brand themselves as revolutionary are just cynically stoking chaos for more nefarious purposes.
2. I've never once heard anyone say anything like that about it
  - Ditto! This is the first time I'm ever hearing it.

All I've ever seen about it is it's not as good as the other two and no one could understand Bane.
3. Absolutely braindead take.

This movie is flawed, more so than the first two.

But is also rousing , inspirational stuff at times. Bale is terrific as usual , playing a broken man who manages to find hope and happiness again.

It is a deeply moving film to a nerd of a certain age like myself
  - Totally feel you, man. TDKR has flaws but that ending hits hard. Bale’s broken Bruce finding hope again is pure heart. Nerd tears every time he climbs out the pit. Epic finish to the trilogy.
4. Apparently it is either that:
- A) Only a couple of leftists have said this.
- B) This is what someone has claimed that leftists have said this.
5. Given the context of the time it felt like it was an exaggerated critique of occupy and reactions to the GFC  Storming the prison, the trials combined with the eat the rich stuff was very French revolution-y and there was nothing really else at the time you could try to connect that to. 

Maybe I'm wrong but that's how it felt to me at the time.

## 2. Detroit is finally getting its Robocop statue. What city deserves a statute of a famous movie character next?

**Score:** 1077
**Body:** Robocop (1987)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ron Burgundy - San Diego
  - Go fuck yourself, San Diego.
2. Debbie in Dallas. She did the whole city after all.
3. Omar Little in Baltimore
4. The Blues Brothers in Chicago
5. King Kong in NYC
  - Snake Plissken for NY
6. This reminds me of that Bill Burr rant about Philadelphia, where the city put up a statue to Rocky Balboa (a fictional character) before Joe Frazier (a real boxer) because they couldn't handle the fact that Joe Frazier was Black.
7. Albuquerque needs a Walter white statue
  - They already do
https://newmexicotravelguy.com/breaking-bad-statue/

## 3. Guillermo del Toro rocks!!

**Score:** 512
**Body:** Loved it or not??

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This just makes me want to see more movies about polar exploration! Amundsen and Scott and the race to the South Pole, Franklin's lost expedition, Andree's Arctic balloon, the Italia airship crash... So many real life adventures and drama happened in the most inhospitable regions on the planet
  - If you haven’t already watched it - you should check out season 1 of *’The Terror’.*

It’s horror themed, but based on a true story of a ship trying to find a passage through the Arctic during this time period.
    - I've already seen that! Love that one. I really like Jared Harris
    - Season 1 goes hard as fuck, shame about s2
2. what movie? please include in title or description in future
  - Frankenstein
3. Shelley wrote the creature to be repulsive to humans, though his features weren't necessarily the problem. She did this to indicate his lack of a soul. del Toro changed the relationship between the creature and Elizabeth to add a sexual element. This movie reminded me of "The Shape of Water" in that regard. Maybe he's got a thing for women being sexually attracted to repulsive creatures.
  - Many women are attracted to repulsive creatures. Many romance novels have the male lead be a human/animal hybrid
    - Half man, half bear, half pig….
      - And is super cereal

## 4. What’s the worst movie you have ever sat through?

**Score:** 278
**Body:** Tommy Wiseau from the movie “The Room”

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oh hai Mark!
  - so bad. but so good at how bad it is 😂😭
    - Saw a midnight show knowing next to nothing about it with a interactive audience and it was a top 3 movie theater experience.
  - My son and I met Wiseau and Greg Sestero at a local screening of the movie, a few years ago. 

I got him to write “Oh hi, <my name>!” on a Tommy Wiseau t-shirt and autograph it. 

It’s a keeper.
2. WW1984. Couldn't make it through in the theater. Attempted it a second time at home, with my wife. Holy cow I wasn't missing much.
3. I just watched “Christmas Vacation 2: Cousin Eddie’s Island Adventure” for a bad movie podcast I co-host. “The Room” ain’t nothing but a walk in the park.
4. The Room is so bad it’s awesome
Oh hi doggy
5. S. Darko, the "sequel" to Donnie Darko. I want a lobotomy in hopes I forget that it even exists.
6. Emoji movie
7. I watched Neil Breen's Fateful Findings and Twisted Pair back to back. They are movies.

## 5. Netflix CEO Ted Sarandos Says Movie Theater Model Is ‘Outdated’: ‘Most of the Country’ Cannot ‘Walk to a Multiplex’

**Score:** 254

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ah yes, that important American value of "walkability." No retail business has ever survived in this country if it could only be reached by automobile.
  - PR-Ragebait to make it into headlines. Surprise; it worked
2. Most of the country can't walk fuck all anywhere. Costco is doomed!
3. 2 adult and 2 kids at the AMC in Newport Jersey City, NJ to watch Zootopia 2 is $91.96. They have a combo deal if you buy 2 drinks and popcorn it will cost additional $26.36. Convenience fee is $8.67. Service fee is $1.00 and taxes are $8.49. A total of $136.58. If you forget the 2 drinks and popcorn, it comes up to $110.22.

https://www.amctheatres.com/
  - “Americans just aren’t interested in movies anymore.”  



Appreciate you bringing receipts.
  - Damn.
4. Also, people have really big tv's in their homes too.
5. Movies used to be affordable, now minimum wage wouldn’t cover a single ticket, let alone a nice seat with snacks.
  - This is the real reason it’s dying. Movies was something everyone could enjoy. Now 4 tickets can cost you 100 bucks without snacks (at least in my area).
6. Going to the theatre is something I look forward to when I’m able to go which unfortunately isn’t very often but still. Seeing a big blockbuster on the big screen or even an IMAX screen can’t be beat!

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Happy Gilmore 2 is a bad movie that unintentionally serves as a fascinating exploration of Adam Sandler and the nature of populism

**Score:** 664
**Body:** Happy Gilmore 2 is (in my opinion) not a good movie, but I’m not here to write a review. The Happy Gilmore movies are zany comedies that you aren’t really meant to think about. However, with a little critical examination, the sequel is incredibly strange in that it follows the same story beats as the original while completely inverting its themes.

In the original, Happy is framed as an audience surrogate and an outsider to golf culture. Happy had no interest in golf until he, already an adult and failed hockey player, accidentally discovers his natural talent for it. He golfs wearing a hockey jersey, he swears, he gets into brawls, and his fans are typical beer-chugging sports guys. He sticks out like a sore thumb among the professional golf crowd and is only barely given a pass due to his skill.

The antagonist of the original, Shooter McGavin, represents the golf establishment. He regards Happy with a snobbish, elitist attitude, and is disgusted that Happy doesn’t fit the mold of a traditional golfer. Shooter believes that, because he ‘paid his dues’ and came up through the golf world the traditional way, he ‘deserves’ to win the championship over Happy.

So that’s the dynamic of the original. Happy is the relatable “man of the people” who sticks out amongst the preppy stuck-up golfers, and the audience wants to see him triumph over the judgmental old guard Shooter. So how is the sequel different?

Well, the villains of Happy Gilmore 2, the Maxi-Golfers, are the direct antithesis to what Shooter McGavin represented. Maxi-Golf is a start up sports league with a completely different type of golf. What Maxi-Golf actually entails is kept vague up until the climax of the movie, other than it being regarded with contempt and disgust by Happy, Shooter, and all the other established golfers.

For most of the film Maxi-Golf is represented by two characters. The first is Frank Manatee, the founder of Maxi-Golf, who is a straightforward cliched Silicon Valley CEO type (outside of a totally not tiresome running joke about him having bad breath) that needs no further explanation. The other, more interesting character is Billy Jenkins. Billy is initially introduced as an affable new pro golfer. After winning the tournament, Billy reveals that he was a Maxi-Golfer the whole time and uses his clout from winning to force the “real” golfers to a Maxi-Golf tournament that will determine the future of golf, setting up the climax of the film.

Two very important reveals happen afterwards that I must detail. First, in a move that carries incredible symbolic significance (that I don’t think the writers realized), Happy recruits Shooter to play alongside him on team Real Golf in the Maxi-Golf tournament. Second, we finally get to see what Maxi-Golf is. It’s essentially an over-the-top actionized version of golf straight out of Idiocracy. There’s pyrotechnics, loud music, crazy hazards, and gimmicky challenges like golfing off the side of a moving cart. It is very deliberately meant to seem low-brow and moronic.
What makes this so interesting to me is that, by the logic of the original Happy Gilmore, Happy and the ‘Real Golfers’ should be the villains. Happy doesn’t just team up with Shooter McGavin, he HAS BECOME Shooter McGavin. He sees something infiltrating the golf world that is at odds with golf culture, something he looks at as crude and dumb in the same way Shooter looked at him, and he feels the need to knock it down to preserve golf. Whereas the audience was meant to be frustrated by the disrespect shown to Happy in the original, they are meant to laugh along with the disrespect shown to Maxi-Golf. Happy is competing with the Real Golfers in order to pull up the very same ladder he once climbed. At no point does Happy Gilmore 2 demonstrate any awareness that it has completely inverted the first film.

So, what are we to make of this? I’m not entirely sure, and I have two possible conclusions to present:

 My first and more simple conclusion is that the contradiction between movies is unintentional commentary on Adam Sandler himself. The first Happy Gilmore was made early in Sander’s career. To paraphrase Rocky 3, Sandler was young and he was hungry. Like Happy, Sandler was someone from the outside with something to prove. Now that Sandler is thoroughly part of the Hollywood machine, not only as an actor but also the owner of a production company, he can only conceptualize the character Happy as an insider. The scene where Happy is cordially dining with other PGA champions is particularly illustrative. Sandler has gone from a guy on SNL to someone who has a seat at any film industry party or awards show he cares to attend. It’s also worth noting that Happy Gilmore 2 is stuffed with cameos by pro-golfers, something that wouldn’t be possible if the movie alienated the PGA and LIV Golf by showing them as villains. Creating Maxi-Golf may have been artistically motivated by a desire for access and a need to play nice with giants in another industry.

My second and more speculative conclusion is that the movies reflect shifting politics and the right-wing co-opting of populism. Happy is a populist figure in both movies. He is the audience surrogate, a relatable normal-ish guy in need of money. In the original he enters the golf world as a “man of the people” and butts heads with rich snobs because he refuses to follow the etiquette of their insular society, attracting a fanbase of typical rowdy sports fans along the way. In Happy Gilmore 2, Happy is once again broke and golfing to make money, but this time his populism sides with the golf establishment rather than against. Golf is under attack by a new outside force, and it’s up to Happy to defend the sanctity of golf (and we all love golf don’t we guys). Populism is a style and it can be used to support anything, even two exact opposite ideas. Populism meant being yourself and sticking it to the man. Now it means keeping the ‘freaks’ from polluting culture and making sure fellow ‘normals’ stay in charge.

I will add here that I found Maxi-Golf and its portrayal as a brand new, brightly colored, culturally destructive force made me vaguely uncomfortable. I want to be very clear that I am not accusing Happy Gilmore 2 or its creators of being bigoted. I have no objectionable content to point to. However, it gives off strange vibes, as if it’s hate propaganda that had several words changed in the script Mad Libs style to remove all the bigotry, if that makes any sense. Like if you tasked a bunch of transphobes to make a comedy movie with trans villains, they would end up making Happy Gilmore 2 but without the golfing. On that note, I haven’t watched Lady Ballers and probably never will, but if by chance anyone here has seen both movies, I’d be interested to know if there are any parallels between the two.

Thanks for reading my ramblings. I’m curious if anyone had similar thoughts, or came to different conclusions, or flat out thinks I’m wrong. I’d love to know your thoughts.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I haven't seen the second movie, but now I'm interested. Excellent commentary. 

I think another example of this is the Snyder remake of Dawn of the Dead, which I read as a thematic reversal of the first. In Romero's, the mall and its shoppers are overtly criticized. Its a corrupting force and the zombies are not some strange unknowable, they are clearly somewhat regular people. There are shots where they are essentially going about their routine, doing the same things they would non-zombie. The mall, consumerism, has made them brain dead. They aren't particularly threatening, but the threat is that you would join/become brain dead too.

In Snyder's, the mall (consumerism), is not corrupting but saves the protagonists. It keeps them safe from the zombies, who unlike in Romero's, are terrifying and confusing. They are a force from the outside and the mall is a sanctuary. Its a defense of western-style capitalism rather than a criticism of it.

Now unlike Happy Gilmore, these are different directors. I am intrigued by the thematic shift you identified. I'd guess its probably unintentional but its really interesting. But like Happy Gilmore, both the films are responding to different times. Romero saw the spread of suburban culture and consumerism and criticized it. Snyder made his films at a time when the Happy 90s had died a tragic death and the United States was fighting two wars overseas,  ostensibly "defending western values." His zombies are not our friends and neighbors but "terrorists" who want to destroy everything with no reason or logic.
  - Not to hijack this unrelated thread, but it's good to read someone acknowledge this about Snyder's Dawn of the Dead. In horror circles it's generally accepted that the remake is as-good if not better than Romero's. And, sure, technically it's a hell of a movie. But the way it uses the shape of Romero's movie to express, either intentionally or not, a reactionary worldview is something I rarely have seen discussed. Aside from what you are pointing out, just looking at who survives and how they die is very telling. How much of that comes from James Gunn's script and how much was further adapted by Snyder is an interesting question; Gunn seems like he would be on Romero's wavelength, while Snyder can't help but speak to his Randian, uber-mench reactionary instincts. And thrills and excitement aside that movie left a bad taste in my mouth.
    - Well, that's who Snyder is. Watchmen had the same issue and, lest we forget, he adapted 300, which is the quintaessential reactionary movie that enhances every negative reactionary aspect about the source comic.
  - Themeatic shifts in remakes is somewhat inevitable. Even though it's a shot for shot remake, Gus Van Sant's Psycho feels like a cotton candy compared to the cold, rather austere Hitchcock classic. Van Sant even pointed out he was proving that you cannot remake something and expect it to hit the same.


Though on that, I rather like how Invasion of the Body Snatchers remakes echo the uneasy fears people have at the time. 70s with the rise in Christian nationalism and stagflation. 00s and the influence of social media on discourse. I'd imagine today one based on AI is already on the cards, but a more interesting one might be looking at how polarised political discourse is driven by fear of the other...


Anyway, back to Gilmore 2, it was a clone made with Netflix money. The popularist shift seemed almost inevitable given the current Zeitgeist. Really what would be interesting is if it had been a daft backpack trip around Scotland, and Gilmore discovering Scottish ancestry in common with McGavin, and with that the pair realise their rivalry is historically based. 
    - I think you might be interested in the show Pluribus. It might be hard to explain why without getting into details that come after the first episode. Also, the exact premise of the show can kind of be considered a spoiler to the first episode, so if anyone doesn't want to read that, just go watch the pilot for yourselves.

Otherwise: >!It is essentially a new take on the idea of "body snatchers", but the body snatchers are a hive mind who act in a sort of benign benevolent way towards the Protag, who is mysteriously immune. And even though creator Vince Gilligan says AI was not on his mind when writing the show, there are a lot of parallels to be made.!<
2. Interesting thoughts. I'd counter that Happy still represents the man of the people. Even though he is competing against Maxi Golf, he still is an outsider to traditional golf. He still plays the same way. He still dresses like a slob. When he goes back to playing again, he doesn't go to some posh country club, he goes to a run down muni tract and plays with a bunch of stoners who also act and dress nothing like traditional golfers. On top of that, he has the ultimate real life golf outsider, John Daly, living with him.

I think that the PGA dinner scene that you used to support your premise actually does a solid job of disproving it. When all the PGA players get together at the dinner, they're not acting like Shooter in the first movie, stuck up, tight, and conservative. They're acting like characters in an Adam Sandler's friends in an Adam Sandler movie, goofy and immature. Hell, Ricky Fowler makes a that's what she said joke about a fork being up Schauffele's ass. It seemed apparent to me that they were using Liv Golf as a model for their antagonist. I believe this point is driven home by the fact that Happy's team is made up of the two best PGA players and the two best Liv players.

Then, the final shot to save traditional golf is made by the ultimate outsider, Oscar, who ironically and presciently is played by Bad Bunny. If anything, Happy Gilmore isn't telling us that freaks are polluting the normal's culture, it's telling us that freaks are the new normal. All of that taken together, I think if we are really going to look this deeply into the socio-political messaging of Happy Gilmore 2, what it is telling us that freaks should be a part of normal society and that normal society should be a little more freaky. But we also have to be aware of going too far, like Maxi Golf. If Maxi is an analogy for Liv Golf, one of Liv's biggest supporters is a certain dipshit in the White House. Maxi Golf is the MAGA movement and we, the freaks and the normals, need to come together to come together and destroy it before it destroys the WORLD!

Then again, maybe they wrote the script in two hours drunk from chasing scotch with triple espressos and just needed a plot point antagonist and Maxi Golf is what they came up with.

I can't believe I just spent 15 minutes on this.
  - Interesting. So Happy's influence helped remedy the elitism in golf culture and by the time of Happy Gilmore 2 the PGA has become what it should have been all along? That is an angle I hadn't considered and makes a lot of sense.
  - Totally agree with this. I think Maxi Golf is meant to broadly parallel the infiltration of insidious corporate culture, with tacky spectacle replacing the real appeal of golf as a sport. Sort of like some parallel for enshittification. I don't think the movie really portrays Happy as 'the institution' and I definitely don't think the Maxi-Golfers are portrayed as underdog outsiders - categorically the opposite, since the narrative conflict comes from the fact that their artificially enhanced abilities vastly outmatch the 'real' athletes, which are Happy and his crew. I found it quite touching that Happy and Shooter ultimately allied together (despite their differences) to defend the value of 'real' sportsmanship, and I think it's definitely significant that the final shot was made by Oscar. Honestly, the idea that HG2 is somehow in favour of conservative values seems to me like a misreading. I think it makes a broader point about real human values, and the celebration of organic athletic ability (which is central to all sports).
3. This is a fantastic write up and thought exercise regarding movies that the vast majority of people don’t want to seriously engage with.  I haven’t seen Happy Gilmore 2 but I think I’ll watch it this weekend.

A quick thought before I watch the sequel:
The original is classic Slobs vs Snobs.  Happy is portrayed as a regular guy who at the end of the day is just trying to play the game that we are familiar with.
It sounds like Maxi-Golf isn’t that at all.  Sure they are “outsiders” but not every outsider is a Good Guy Underdog.  It’s interesting that you bring up bigotry/transphobia.  My thoughts are going towards Spotify, Netflix, Big Streaming etc.  They were “outsiders” to the culture industry, and people are clearly hooked on their product.  Are they underdogs?  Are they making culture better?
4. I think the key difference is Happy was an outsider as a person. But he played the sport on its existing terms, albeit in an unorthodox way. 

This isn’t the case with Maxi, which was an attempted wholesale replacement of a sport by something different, and with all the cynicism that comes with Silicon Valley. 

So it stands to reason that Shooter and Happy team up. 

It’s like how family members will fight each other but unite when someone external antagonises a family member. Theres an old joke that the only way humanity will have peace is if we get invaded by aliens. 

The principle is the same: we fight over our differences until an external factor makes us unite over our similarities.

## 3. Werner Herzog as a documentarian

**Score:** 36
**Body:** Like more than a few people on this subreddit (I assume), I've been diving into the Criterion Channel's Werner Herzog retrospective. I've been really enjoying it and thought that Herzog is very much worthy of an r/truefilm thread.

I'd like to focus on the documentaries.

Compared to traditional documentaries, two aspects of Herzog's documentary work stand out. First and most obviously, Herzog himself as narrator, interviewer and overall host, one very willing to bring his own personality and point of view to the film. A lot of documentarians are not active presences in their films, but Herzog definitely is. A lot of your enjoyment of these films probably depends on how much you enjoy Herzog as your 'tour guide.'

Second is the close thematic connection between Herzog's documentaries and his fiction films. The Wheel of Time (2003) is about a literal pilgrimage but many (most?) of Herzog's films are about metaphorical pilgrimages, about quests, whether it's Timothy Treadwell seeking a life with bears or Fitzcarraldo transporting a steamboat across dry land. (Or, indeed, Werner Herzog the filmmaker traveling to remote, dangerous locations to make films.)

Grizzly Man is probably Herzog's most famous documentary and possibly his best. I appreciate how it tells the story of an eccentric, possibly misguided outsider in a way that doesn't feel either exploitative or condescending. And while "X is a film that only Y could make" is kind of a cliche of film criticism, I'm not sure that any other filmmaker would be able to see as much of themselves in Treadwell as Herzog did.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. his [manifesto](https://walkerart.org/magazine/minnesota-declaration-truth-documentary-cinema-1999/) is worth a read and might shed some light.

i would summarize it as this:

capital "T" Truth in documentary doesn't exist.   as soon as you introduce a camera, sequence something, edit something, choose to show something, choose not to show something, you are depicting something else.

therefore: "There are deeper strata of truth in cinema, and there is such a thing as poetic, ecstatic truth. It is mysterious and elusive, and can be reached only through fabrication and imagination and stylization."
2. The first Herzog film I saw was Little Dieter Needs To Fly and the filmmaker was everpresent in the documentary. He traveled to Laos with Dieter Dengler and recreated the events with local actors. Herzog made Rescue Dawn a decade later which is a full dramatization of Dieter's story.
3. Into the Abyss is depressing but fascinating. It’s about a young guy’s last few weeks on death row. It feels so antithetical to Herzog’s style as a director. It’s not sensationalist true crime stuff but a portrait of lives shattered. Obviously, it’s pretty depressing but fascinating and moving too.
  - For me the greatest achievement of “Into the Abyss” is how subtly and persuasively it makes the argument that the death penalty is inhumane because it sullies the humanity of everyone involved in the process — from the inmates, to the warden, the guards, the chaplain and the victims’ survivors.
    - Yeah and he articulates it so well. Even to the guy on death row. He’s pretty much like “I don’t like you, I don’t like what you did but I believe all human life has value including yours and your victim’s.”
  - My favourite Herzog documentary by far. I'm not sure if I agree that it's antithetical in relation to his other documentary work. The common theme is extreme experience - man venturing out to encounter some gargantuan force of nature that has the capacity to destroy/consume him. I can't really be bothered with watching men messing around with volcanos or bears, but when the force is something universal like death it's a different matter. In addition it's superbly well made - quiet, respectful, compassionate, and very powerful.
  - I've honestly never seen that film.

One good thing about filmmakers like Herzog is that their lengthy discographies mean that you always have something new to discover.
4. I saw Lessons of Darkness in a movie theater, was an amazing viewing -
Though I wouldn’t really call that a documentary- a lot of his doc films he invents stories and scenarios…and he’s been open about it I believe— I can see how some purists could see them not as docs…something of a hybrid film, or personal essay on a subject.
  - Considering the fact that even nature documentaries are heavily edited in a way to form a narrative rather than attempt to show “objective reality”, I’d still consider Lessons in Darkness a documentary.
5. I just watched The Dark Glow of the Mountains and you could hear Herzog speaking German but it was dubbed into English with some generic announcer voice that really took away from the film. Granted, it's one of his more obscure documentaries.

A pilgrimage is an interesting way to think of Herzog's work. I thought of them as people living on the fringes of civilization - either facing nature or death or time - always pondering if there's more out there that we don't know about.

## 4. Sentimental Value - Ending scene

**Score:** 11
**Body:** Hello.  I just recently watched Joachim Trier’s Sentimental Value and I really enjoyed the film. The film is a great portrait of this family and how art is used for connection and reconciliation. The ending conversation with Agnes and Nora was amazing and really moved me. 

But one thing I can’t get fully understand is why Nora wants to act in Gustav’s movie. I get that the film is sort of a mirror to Nora and reflects her life in some way and it allows her to stop running away from her past and confront it. But I feel there is something bigger to this that I am not understanding. Can anyone explain this to me?

Thank you!
 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For me, I think that over the course of the film - she comes to realise that she's never going to have the relationship or communication style she wishes she had with him, because he is so stuck in his ways.

So acting in the film is to some extent her coming to terms with that, and trying to communicate with him in the only way that he's capable, even though it's flawed - so I think it is a bittersweet ending
  - I also think she comes to a recognition that he did, somehow “see” her, which was something she very clearly needed. She talks several times about how he can’t have any idea who she is or how good of an actor she is because he was never there or couldn’t look past the clutter of the stage show to see her actual performance. But he bypasses those things and did somehow understand something true about her. 

So I think she accepts that and to some extent reevaluates her expectations. I also think that talk with Agnes is crucial for her to reframe her perspective of her own world. When Agnes discloses that she needed Nora to provide safety and stability, it’s a recognition of sisterly thanks and sacrifice, but it also gives Nora a wholly new frame to view her own life, which offers an opportunity for a new emotional perspective of her father’s perception of her.
  - Thank you!
2. Okay, so I realized that while I was responding to your question, I actually need a bit more time to flesh out the ideas in the film, but just for starters:

Throughout the movie, Nora says how she and her father can't communicate.

Whenever they do, it's awkward. It kind of reminds me of when exes communicate. (It's also interesting that early on when they meet at the restaurant to talk, Gustav comments how the waitress thinks that he and Nora must be a couple; and later on, her nephew admits he wants to marry her when he's older. Perhaps all of this is simply to keep the idea of Relationships in mind, whether romantic, platonic, or familial.)

At first, Nora refuses to read Gustav's screenplay. But ultimately at Agnes' behest, Nora gives in and reads the thing in one sitting. Gustav has communicated.

Nora is an actress. Gustav is a director. This is a natural pairing, and so too is Father & Daughter, and yet they fail in the latter, but exceed in the former, albeit independently.

The script is ultimately about Nora. The mention of the suicide attempt, both she and Agnes were wondering how he could have known. This speaks to the almost spiritual or deeper link between family. Even though there may have been a failure in communication, both possess the empathy required to be masters of their craft. And it's through their craft (all artifice) that they can finally communicate. But it's funny because when she finishes the scene, she and Gustav just stare at each other and laugh and smile, they don't say anything to each other.
  - Thanks for your comment! Really insightful
3. I actually disagree with the comments here who think Gustav is just an incredibly intuitive writer. Rather, I think the film is constantly hinting that not everything is as it seems with Gustav. 

We know that Gustav is the son of a woman who suffered a lot of trauma, and ended up killing herself. So he was absent a parent growing up. Similarly, Nora was absent a parent growing up. You know the consequences for Nora in particular have been pretty bad, culminating in her suicide attempt. We know about Gustav that the only times he ever calls Nora is when he's drunk. We also see him get drunk in the film, and indeed, he ends up calling Nora. He gets so drunk towards the end of the film that he ends up in the hospital, nearly killing himself. 

And here's the clincher: Nora reads the screenplay, and asks her sister if she had told Gustav about Nora's suicide attempt. She had not. So how did Gustav know? 

There's a very telling shot in the last third of the film, a surreal shot that shows Gustav's face overlapping the two daughters, in a way that is not dissimilar from the famous shot in Persona. It seems to have the implication that Gustav and his two children are more like than they think. 

So again, how did Gustav know what it was like to be in a suicidal mindset? The answer, as I see it, is that Gustav himself suffers the same trauma, as inherited from his mother, as past to his daughter. So what Nora saw in that screenplay was not just herself, it was her father. He's also been suicidal. And so Nora sees herself in Gustav, and Gustav sees himself in Nora.

It's because of that recognition, I think, that Nora agrees to do the movie.
  - Wow. I really like your interpretation. Really good analysis.
4. LOTS OF SPOILERS, DON'T SAY YOU WEREN'T WARNED!

I think it was a combination of things. we saw the scene where she and her sister started to have warm feelings towards their dad when he was in the hospital so I think she started to forgive him then. I think she also realized he was right and she was only going to get so far in her career playing roles in the classics in the theater. he is after all a talented and esteemed director and it certainly would boost her career and get her unstuck from a life that wasn't making her happy. I think she ultimately was flattered that he wrote a role that good just for her and saw it as an act of love.

from a screenwriting perspective, it was a nice bookend to how the movie started with the other sister starring in one of his early movies. it's heavily implied that this is probably going to be his last movie so having his other daughter in it was a nice way to come full circle.

what I liked is that the family is supportive because they're all there on the set but it's clear there's still a little distance and everything isn't tied up neatly in a bow,  there is still work to be done to repair their relationships.
  - Thank you!

## 5. Joachim Trier’s Sentimental Value is excellent art about art

**Score:** 8
**Body:** i’ve recently read a few fiction books that are about artists and writers (Bel Canto, How To Be Both, The Friend) and found them all a little insufferable in how much more interested they are in self-aggrandizement for the author than actually examining art and whether it’s capable of changing people and the world. i also saw Hamnet and it didn’t totally land for me, so i was beginning to think i’m just totally out on art about art but Sentimental Value is such a good film, and it really moved me. 

I think it works so well because of how nuanced a character Gustav is allowed to be. i’ve seen stellan skarsgard and joachim trier both say in interviews that they didn’t want him to be a cliche of an old fool or a one note bad father, and you can really tell how much care they put into his characterization. He’s such a good realistic yet forgiving viewpoint on what successful artists and filmmakers are really like - his work is kind of a self indulgent exercise where he gets to manipulate his own universe, yet it’s also his primary way of exploring the love he has for his daughters that he struggles to express. this anxiety between the good and bad elements of artistic expression is really neat to see explored and Trier does such a good job with it. Makes me think of how so many of his films have little interludes that follow back all the generations of a character’s family and how comfortable he is showing that you can’t pick and choose the best of people, you have to take them all or nothing. I think you can view Gustav as a stand in for filmmaking as a whole, and how Trier might feel about it. In his New Yorker profile he talks about how film history is full of narcissists who were horrible to the people in their lives, even if they made great art. but he’s obviously a huge film nerd, and the medium has clearly had a profound emotional effect on his life. filmmaking has some pretty ugly skeletons in its closet but it’s also one of the best examples of human transcendence. Trier being clear eyed about both of these realities makes the weathered optimism of his work hit even harder.

I felt really personally connected to Sentimental Value and wrote a review about it if you’re interested!

https://open.substack.com/pub/stalewine/p/the-value-of-sentiment?r=h4dad&utm_medium=ios
